__inputs.name: DS_PROMETHEUS
__inputs.label: Prometheus
__inputs.description: Prometheus data source scraping the OTLP -> Prometheus stream that carries mdk's OTel metrics.

description: mdk (movate-cli) golden-signal ops dashboard over the OTel metrics the runtime/worker emit (ADR 031 D2). Prescriptive layer: triage flow at the top, per-chart triage notes, SLO threshold annotations matching infra/azure/modules/monitor-alerts.bicep, and drill-down links to Jaeger + Azure Monitor Workbooks. Metric instrument names: mdk.jobs.completed, mdk.job.duration_ms, mdk.jobs.in_flight, mdk.run.tokens, mdk.run.cost_usd, plus the DB connection-pool gauges mdk.db.pool.size, mdk.db.pool.idle, mdk.db.pool.in_use, mdk.db.pool.waiting, mdk.db.pool.max (ADR 034 D3). See dashboards/README.md.

options.content:
## mdk - golden signals

**What this shows.** The four golden signals for the mdk runtime/worker (latency, throughput + errors, saturation, and cost) sourced from OTel metrics emitted at the runtime edge (`src/movate/tracing/metrics.py`).

**Normal range** (steady-state, single tenant, mixed workload):

- Latency p95 < 2s, p99 < 5s (the **high-latency-p95** SLO alert fires at p95 > 30000ms; see `infra/azure/modules/monitor-alerts.bicep`).
- Error rate (`status != success`) < 1% (SLO alert: **high-error-rate** > 10%).
- Dead-letter count = 0 (SLO alert: **deadletter-spike** > 1 in 15m).
- Cost burn flat (no order-of-magnitude jumps).
- DB pool `in_use/max` < 0.7; `waiting` = 0.

**Triage flow.** Walk these rows top-to-bottom; the first row that's anomalous is usually the proximate cause.

```mermaid
graph TD
  A[Page fired or SLO breach] --> B{Latency row red?}
  B -- yes --> B1[Open p95-by-kind panel] --> B2[Jaeger: filter by slow kind, inspect agent.execute children]
  B -- no  --> C{Throughput / errors row red?}
  C -- yes --> C1{status == dead_letter?}
  C1 -- yes --> C2[Inspect failed runs in Langfuse; check provider 5xx + retry budget]
  C1 -- no  --> C3[Correlate error spike with deploy revision annotations]
  C -- no  --> D{Saturation row red?}
  D -- yes --> D1[in_flight rising without throughput? -> workers stuck on a slow tool/LLM]
  D -- no  --> E{Cost row red?}
  E -- yes --> E1[Tokens spiked? -> KB chunk-size regression; mdk eval drift]
  E -- no  --> F{DB pool row red?}
  F -- yes --> F1[in_use/max > 0.9 or waiting > 0 -> raise pod count or pool_max; rerun mdk doctor]
  F -- no  --> G[Check upstream: collector / Prometheus scrape gap?]
```

If Mermaid does not render in your Grafana, the same flow as a list:

1. Latency red -> p95-by-kind -> Jaeger by kind.
2. Throughput/errors red -> dead-letter? then Langfuse, else deploy correlation.
3. Saturation red -> workers stuck on slow tool/LLM.
4. Cost red -> token spike -> KB / eval drift.
5. DB pool red -> raise pods or pool_max; rerun `mdk doctor`.

**Cross-surface.** Same metrics rendered KQL-side in `infra/azure-monitor/workbooks/operator.workbook.json` (Azure Monitor). The Azure SLO alert names referenced below all live in `infra/azure/modules/monitor-alerts.bicep`.

panels.description: Job wall-clock duration percentiles from the mdk.job.duration_ms histogram (claim -> terminal status). p50 / p95 / p99 over the histogram buckets.
panels.title: Job duration p50 / p95 / p99 (red line = high-latency-p95 SLO @ 30s)

panels.description: Job latency p95 split by job kind (the `kind` attribute on mdk.job.duration_ms).

links.title: Open Jaeger - filter by slowest kind

panels.title: Job duration p95 by kind (red line = high-latency-p95 SLO @ 30s)

options.content:
**What this measures.** End-to-end wall-clock from job claim to terminal status (`mdk.job.duration_ms`), and the same split by `kind`.

**Normal.** p95 < 2s, p99 < 5s under steady load. The red threshold line at **30000ms** mirrors the **`*-high-latency-p95`** SLO alert in `infra/azure/modules/monitor-alerts.bicep`.

**If you see X.**

- `p95 > 5s` -> check the per-tenant rate-limit dashboard; in Jaeger, search `service=movate-runtime operation=agent.execute minDuration=5s` and look for slow `agent.turn[N]` or `skill.<name>` children. If the agent is KB-heavy, inspect `kb_search` spans for slow `embed`/`rerank` stages.
- `p95 > 30s` (SLO line) -> **`*-high-latency-p95`** is firing; treat as an SEV-2. Correlate timing with deploy revisions in Azure Container Apps (or with `mdk deploy` Activity Log entries).
- `p99 spike, p50 flat` -> tail-latency only; usually a provider hiccup. Inspect provider attribute on `agent.execute` in Jaeger.

**ADR refs.** ADR 013 (tracing baseline) / ADR 015 (Langfuse) / ADR 020 (Azure exporter). **Alert rule.** `*-high-latency-p95`.

panels.description: Job completion throughput (jobs/sec) by terminal status, from mdk.jobs.completed (`status` = success / error / safety_blocked / dead_letter).
panels.title: Job throughput by status

panels.description: Error rate = non-success terminal jobs / all terminal jobs over the window (mdk.jobs.completed). Dead-letters are the `status=dead_letter` slice.
panels.title: Error rate & dead-letter rate (red line = high-error-rate SLO @ 10%)

options.content:
**What this measures.** Job completion rate split by terminal `status`, and the non-success / dead-letter ratio over the rate window. Source: `mdk.jobs.completed`.

**Normal.** Steady non-zero `success` line; `error` < 1% of total; `dead_letter` rate = 0. Red threshold line on the right panel = **10%** (the **`*-high-error-rate`** SLO from `infra/azure/modules/monitor-alerts.bicep`).

**If you see X.**

- `error rate > 1%` -> check Azure Container Apps Activity Log for the deploy window; correlate with revision rollouts (annotate the chart with deploy time via Grafana's manual annotation if you have that wired).
- `error rate > 10%` (SLO line) -> **`*-high-error-rate`** is firing; treat as SEV-1. Open Jaeger filter `error=true`; pivot on the `provider` attribute on `agent.execute`.
- `dead_letter rate > 0` -> the **`*-deadletter-spike`** SLO fires on any non-zero; inspect failed runs in Langfuse, check provider 5xx + retry budget, and confirm Sentinel poison-message handling isn't suppressed.
- `503 errors` -> suspect runtime cold start; check Container Apps revision restart events (use the `eval-and-drift` workbook's Container Apps section, or `AppEvents | where Type contains 'restart'`).

**ADR refs.** ADR 013 / ADR 019 (queue tracing) / ADR 020. **Alert rules.** `*-high-error-rate`, `*-deadletter-spike`.

panels.description: In-flight jobs being dispatched by workers (claimed, not yet terminal) from the mdk.jobs.in_flight up-down counter. A proxy for queue/worker saturation. (A true queue-depth instrument is deferred to item #27 and not yet emitted as a metric.)
panels.title: Jobs in flight (saturation)

options.content:
**What this measures.** Up-down counter of currently-claimed-but-not-terminal jobs (proxy for in-pipe depth; true queue depth is deferred per ADR 031). Source: `mdk.jobs.in_flight`.

**Normal.** Rises with traffic, falls with throughput. Steady-state value approximates `(arrival_rate * avg_duration)`. No alert rule yet (true queue depth is item #27).

**If you see X.**

- `in_flight rising, throughput flat or falling` -> workers stuck. Pull Jaeger with `minDuration=10s`; the long traces will reveal the stuck `skill.<name>` or LLM call.
- `in_flight stuck non-zero with zero throughput` -> consumer side dead. Check Container Apps worker scale-out (KEDA), confirm `mdk worker` pods are healthy, look at the **`*-availability-no-traffic`** alert if API stalled.
- `flat at 0 with traffic on the API side` -> claim path broken (locks held, storage outage). Inspect the DB pool row.

**ADR refs.** ADR 019 (queue + W3C context) / ADR 031 (instrument catalog). **Alert rule (related).** `*-availability-no-traffic` (API-side stall).

panels.description: LLM spend rate (USD/hour) from the mdk.run.cost_usd counter, by tenant.
panels.title: LLM cost rate (USD/hr) by tenant

panels.description: Cumulative LLM cost (USD) over the selected range from mdk.run.cost_usd, and cumulative tokens from mdk.run.tokens.
panels.title: Cumulative cost & tokens (range)

options.content:
**What this measures.** Per-tenant LLM spend rate (USD/hr derived from `mdk.run.cost_usd`) and cumulative cost + tokens over the dashboard window. Both counters only carry the `tenant` attribute; per-agent / per-provider splits live on `agent.execute` span attributes (query Jaeger / Langfuse).

**Normal.** Flat / proportional to throughput. No alert rule today (cost is observed, not paged; the relevant ADR is 017 for budget posture).

**If you see X.**

- `cost per agent runs away` -> check the `mdk-cost.json` dashboard's `mdk.run.tokens` panel for the same tenant. If input tokens spiked, look for a KB chunk-size regression (run `mdk eval` against that agent's regression set). If output tokens spiked, suspect prompt/temperature drift; cross-link to `mdk eval` for the agent.
- `USD/hr step-change with no token change` -> provider price change or model swap. Confirm against the `provider` and `model_override` attributes on recent `agent.execute` spans.
- `cost going to zero` -> usage instrument not firing; check `record_run_usage` wiring or `MOVATE_TRACE_SINK`.

**ADR refs.** ADR 017 (cost posture) / ADR 015 (Langfuse for per-run drilldown). **Alert rule.** None today (observed only).

panels.title: DB connection pool (mdk.db.pool.size / idle / in_use / waiting / max, ADR 034 D3)

panels.description: Per-pod asyncpg connection-pool saturation (ADR 034 D3). mdk.db.pool.in_use (checked-out) and mdk.db.pool.idle vs the configured per-pod ceiling mdk.db.pool.max, plus total open mdk.db.pool.size. Under KEDA autoscale, sum(mdk_db_pool_in_use) across pods approaching the Postgres max_connections is the connection-exhaustion early warning the `mdk doctor` capacity check (ADR 034 D1) does statically.
panels.title: Pool connections: in-use / idle / open vs ceiling

panels.description: Callers blocked waiting for a free pool connection (mdk.db.pool.waiting). A sustained non-zero value means the per-pod pool is the bottleneck.
panels.title: Pool acquire waiters (saturation; red line = any waiter is anomalous)
Sub-panel: triage notes (DB pool, ADR 034 D3)

options.content:
**What this measures.** Per-pod asyncpg pool composition (`mdk.db.pool.size`/`idle`/`in_use`/`max`) and acquire-queue waiters (`mdk.db.pool.waiting`). Sampled at OTel collection time.

**Normal.** `in_use/max` < 0.7; `waiting` = 0. Pool gauges are flat/zero on the local SQLite backend.

**If you see X.**

- `in_use/max > 0.9` -> pod is close to pool exhaustion. Cross-reference total `sum(mdk_db_pool_in_use)` vs Azure Postgres `max_connections` (header capacity = `N_pods * pool_max`). Run `mdk doctor` (ADR 034 D1) for the static math.
- `waiting > 0 sustained` -> ceiling too tight. Raise `pool_max` (per-pod) OR add pod headroom; do NOT raise both blindly (you'll trip the Postgres ceiling).
- `in_use rising while throughput flat` -> connection leak in a recently added storage call site. Bisect against the latest deploy.

**ADR refs.** ADR 034 (D1 static doctor check, D3 live gauges). **Alert rule.** None today; the live signal precedes a `503` on the runtime which would trip `*-high-error-rate`.

title: mdk - golden signals (jobs, latency, errors, cost)
